title: Today's Tech Brief: People are leaving a lot of weird stuff in their robotaxis
summary: A look at strange items left behind in autonomous vehicles and what it reveals about their use.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for People are leaving a lot of weird stuff in their robotaxis
imageCaption: People are leaving a lot of weird stuff in their robotaxis (theverge.com)

body.text: I've been digging into some interesting developments in the world of autonomous vehicles, specifically robotaxis. It seems people are leaving some pretty weird stuff behind in these self-driving cars. I mean, who wouldn't want to know what kind of things people leave behind in a car that's driving itself?

According to a report from The Verge, Uber's annual Lost and Found Index has included items left behind in autonomous vehicles for the first time. The list is quite something - we're talking wigs, wedding dresses, phones, wallets, and who knows what else. It's like people think these cars have a magic cleaning crew or something.

But what's really interesting here is that these items aren't just being left behind in any old car. These are vehicles that are being driven by complex software and sensors, with no human intervention. To me, this says a lot about how comfortable people are getting with this technology. I mean, if you're willing to leave your grandma's antique vase in a self-driving car, you must trust it pretty implicitly.

People are leaving a lot of weird stuff in their robotaxis, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Transportation News Autonomous Cars People are leaving a lot of weird stuff in their robotaxis Uber’s annual Lost and Found Index includes items left behind in autonomous vehicles for the first time. Uber’s annual Lost and Found Index includes items left behind in autonomous vehicles for the first time. by Andrew J. Hawkins Jun 2, 2026, 12:00 PM UTC Image: ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
